metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-repair backup/snapshot step and a documented rollback procedure (e.g., 'take a store snapshot before running --repair; rollback = restore from snapshot') so that both the rebuild and hard-delete operations are reversible until verified.
findings.finding: The plan includes two destructive/irreversible operations — (1) hard-deleting LWW-scalar orphans (AC#3) and (2) overwriting existing snapshots via rebuild (What/AC#1) — but provides no explicit rollback path, back-out plan, or pre-repair backup step. Human triage acknowledgement in AC#3 does not constitute a reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: What section; AC#1; AC#3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope/What: 'Before remediation, take a full store backup (e.g., cp -r or equivalent). Rollback path: restore from backup if post-repair fsck reveals regressions or incorrect deletions. Hard-deletes are committed only after human sign-off on the triage report.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring a dry-run mode (no writes, just reports what would change) and a pre-repair backup/export of all affected snapshot records before any writes are made to the live store.
findings.finding: No backup or rollback plan is specified before the repair tool overwrites live snapshots. If the repair logic has a bug (e.g., incorrect event ordering, wrong .retired file parsing), all 2422 affected snapshots could be overwritten with incorrect data and the original (even if inconsistent) state is unrecoverable.
findings.location: Scope / What section — 'run it against the live store'
findings.suggested_fix: Implement `fsck --repair --dry-run` that logs the before/after diff per snapshot without writing. Require a full export (e.g., rebar export or DB dump of affected IDs) to a safe location before the live run, with a documented restore procedure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the repair path handles tickets that already have an existing (incorrect) SNAPSHOT — A2's compact machinery skips them by design; document whether `fsck --repair` must first delete the bad snapshot, and confirm re-runs are safe after interruption.
findings.finding: The plan does not address resumability/idempotency for the repair path. Critically, the A2 compact machinery skips tickets that already have a SNAPSHOT; since SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT tickets already have (incorrect) snapshots, a naively re-run compact_all would skip all 2422 affected tickets. The plan provides no mechanism to detect already-repaired vs still-corrupt tickets on restart after a partial failure.
findings.location: AC bullet 1 / What section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a mandatory pre-repair step: tag or branch the tracker git repo at current HEAD (e.g. `git tag pre-repair-$(date +%Y%m%d)`) so any repair can be rolled back via `git reset --hard <tag>`, and assert the post-rollback store state (all original events present, no SNAPSHOTs from the repair run).
findings.finding: The plan specifies no back-out path and does not require taking a git branch/tag backup of the live store before beginning destructive repairs. Source events deleted during snapshot rebuild are unrecoverable without a prior git ref. The plan notes LWW-scalar orphans may be 'hard-deleted' and only says 'decision recorded', with no rollback mechanism described if the human triage decision turns out to be wrong.
findings.location: AC bullet 3 / Scope / entire plan
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the run plan: 'Before any mutation: `git -C <tracker> tag pre-repair-YYYYMMDD`. If rollback is needed: `git -C <tracker> reset --hard pre-repair-YYYYMMDD && git push --force-with-lease`. Post-rollback state: all source events restored, repair SNAPSHOTs absent, fsck returns to pre-repair counts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit classification table (or decision rule) mapping each KNOWN_EVENT_TYPE to 'additive' (auto-recovered) vs 'LWW-scalar' (human-triaged), and co-locate it with the repair path spec in the plan.
findings.finding: The orphan disposition policy in AC3 references two categories — 'additive orphans (comment/tag/link)' and 'LWW-scalar orphans (folded siblings hard-deleted pre-A1)' — whose classification vocabulary, mapping rules, and decision protocol are never defined anywhere in the plan or the codebase. An executor implementing the repair path has no way to mechanically classify a given ORPHAN_EVENT file into one category or the other.
findings.location: AC3: 'Orphan disposition policy applied: additive orphans (comment/tag/link) recovered; LWW-scalar orphans (folded siblings hard-deleted pre-A1) triaged by a human, decision recorded.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table to the plan body, e.g.: 'Additive event types (auto-recovered): COMMENT, LINK, UNLINK, TAG_DELTA. LWW-scalar event types (human triage): STATUS, EDIT (non-tag fields). All others: log and skip.' This makes the disposition rule concrete and unambiguous for the implementer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an empirical validation step (e.g., a `--dry-run` mode that prints proposed changes without writing, or a pilot run against a snapshot-copy of the store with before/after fsck verification) before executing against the live store.
findings.finding: The repair path (rebuilding 2422+ snapshots from full ordered event logs including `.retired` files) is a novel, unverified operation against live corrupted data. The plan specifies no empirical validation step — no dry-run mode, no staging/shadow run, no RED test fixture, and no pilot against a subset — before executing against the live store.
findings.location: What / Scope / AC lines 1 & 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add `fsck --repair --dry-run` flag that runs the full repair logic and prints what it *would* change (snapshot diffs, orphan dispositions) without committing writes. Gate the live run on a human review of dry-run output, and record the dry-run fsck count in the before/after log required by AC line 2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that repair writes are atomic (write-to-temp then rename/swap) and the tool is safe to re-run (idempotent): already-repaired snapshots are skipped or re-verification is harmless.
findings.finding: The repair tool has no specified error-handling or idempotency guarantee: if it crashes mid-run (I/O error, process kill, OOM), it may leave some snapshots rebuilt and others not, with no checkpoint or safe-restart mechanism described. Re-running a non-idempotent repair on partially-repaired state could compound corruption.
findings.location: AC / Scope — 'Repair tooling (e.g. an fsck --repair-style rebuild)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a write-tmp-then-atomic-rename pattern per snapshot; record a per-snapshot 'repaired' marker (e.g., in a side-file or within the snapshot metadata) so re-runs skip already-completed entries. Log each repaired snapshot ID with before/after hash for auditability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback/recovery specification: define the idempotency guarantee for re-running the repair on already-fixed tickets, name the concrete mechanism for pausing/resuming the reconciler, and specify what per-ticket error handling looks like (skip-and-log vs. abort).
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for a failed or partially-completed repair sub-step. The plan says the reconciler pass is 'paused during remediation' but does not define what happens if the repair tool crashes mid-run, if an individual ticket rebuild fails, or if the reconciler cannot be paused — leaving the executor with no defined recovery path for an interrupted run against 2422+ tickets.
findings.location: AC4: 'Run coordinated with live writers (reconciler pass paused during remediation).' / Scope: 'a coordinated live run'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the repair mechanism for SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT: specify whether the fix is (a) deleting the surviving source files already listed in each affected SNAPSHOT's `source_event_uuids` (aligning with what `compact.py` does post-write) or (b) replacing the SNAPSHOT — and justify which is chosen and why.
findings.finding: For SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT, the proposed 'rebuild snapshot from full ordered event log' mechanism does not match the actual fault: fsck.py:_check_snapshot() fires because source_event_uuids listed in an existing SNAPSHOT still exist as live .json files (compact wrote the SNAPSHOT but didn't delete the originals). The correct targeted fix is to delete the surviving source files already encoded in the SNAPSHOT — not replace the SNAPSHOT itself. Rebuilding the SNAPSHOT from scratch replaces a structurally valid artifact with unnecessary new state and risks introducing new inconsistencies (e.g. clock skew on the new SNAPSHOT timestamp, or diverging compiled_state if any event was partially applied).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC#1 / What section ('rebuild each affected snapshot from the full ordered event log')
findings.suggested_fix: For SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT: the repair path should iterate affected ticket dirs, read each SNAPSHOT's source_event_uuids, and `os.remove` each source file still present on disk (exactly replicating compact.py:140-144's deletion step that RC1 skipped). This is the minimal, targeted fix with the lowest blast-radius. Only if a SNAPSHOT is itself corrupt or missing should a full rebuild from the event log be needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism to pause the reconciler (e.g. a config flag, a systemd unit stop, or an env var) and add a proving artifact — e.g. a timestamped ops log entry or a process-status check — to AC4 so coordination is verifiable.
findings.finding: AC4 ('Run coordinated with live writers — reconciler pass paused during remediation') is effort-framed and has no proving command and no described mechanism for pausing/unpausing the reconciler, making it unverifiable after the fact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
findings.suggested_fix: State the default: 'Pause via `systemctl stop rebar-reconciler` (or equivalent); verify with `systemctl is-active rebar-reconciler` returning `inactive` before repair starts; resume after fsck confirms 0 counts; record start/stop timestamps in the ops run-log.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing plan for the repair path: specify at least (a) a unit/integration test using a synthetic corrupt fixture covering the happy path AND failure cases (missing/malformed `.retired`, partial failure, idempotency), and (b) a dry-run or staging-copy acceptance criterion that exercises the full rebuild before the live run.
findings.finding: The repair path introduces new logic (rebuild from ordered event log + orphan disposition) but the plan specifies no testing strategy beyond the live production run itself — no unit tests for the rebuild machinery, no dry-run/staging verification on a copy of the corrupt data, and no failure-path coverage (malformed/missing `.retired` files, reconciler fails to pause, partial rebuild failures, idempotency of re-running repair).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all four ACs; Scope section ('Repair tooling … src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py / compact.py')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Repair logic is exercised against a synthetic fixture with known SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE records (including missing .retired files and duplicate events) and produces correct output before the live run.' Add a separate dry-run AC: 'A --dry-run flag (or equivalent) runs the full repair path against a copy of the live store and reports expected changes without mutating data, verified before the coordinated live run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_api.py` (or a dedicated `.retired`-aware reader) to the plan's edit-target list and specify how the repair path reads `.retired` event files that `reduce_ticket` currently excludes.
findings.finding: `reduce_ticket` (in `src/rebar/reducer/_api.py`) explicitly excludes `*.retired` files, so AC-1's requirement to 'read `.retired`' from A1 cannot be satisfied by calling the existing `reduce_ticket` without either modifying that function or adding an alternative event-log reader — neither of which is named in the plan's edit targets.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC-1 ('reads `.retired`') / Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add `src/rebar/reducer/_api.py` (or a new `reducer/_retired.py` helper) as an edit target. Specify that the repair path either (a) passes a flag to `reduce_ticket` to include `.retired` files, or (b) implements a standalone ordered-log reader that merges live and `.retired` events before calling the reducer processors directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a maximum repair window / watchdog timeout after which the reconciler auto-resumes (or an operator is paged), preventing an indefinite pause if the repair tool crashes or hangs.
findings.finding: The plan pauses the reconciler during remediation but does not specify a failsafe auto-resume: if the repair tool crashes or hangs, the reconciler stays paused indefinitely, blocking normal live-store operation.
findings.location: AC line 4 — 'Run coordinated with live writers (reconciler pass paused during remediation)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a max-duration flag to the pause mechanism (e.g., reconciler resumes automatically after N minutes or on SIGTERM of the repair process); include a runbook step for manual resume.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step for AC1 that demonstrates the repair path consumes `.retired` files — e.g., a deterministic test fixture containing a `.retired` segment and an assertion that the rebuilt snapshot matches the expected state including those events.
findings.finding: AC1 ('Repair path rebuilds snapshots from the full ordered log including .retired') has no described proving command — there is no step that verifies the repair path actually reads .retired files as distinct from a non-retired-aware rebuild.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'Verified by: `rebar fsck --repair --dry-run` log output shows `.retired` file(s) opened, OR a unit test fixture with a `.retired` segment asserts the rebuilt snapshot digest equals the expected value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete operator procedure for pausing the reconciler (e.g., the exact git command to set refs/reconciler/gate, or an equivalent mechanism) to the plan's runbook/coordination section.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the remediation will be 'run coordinated with live writers (reconciler pass paused during remediation)' but provides no specification of how the reconciler is paused. The phase-gate mechanism (refs/reconciler/gate via check_phase_gate) exists in code but there is no documented operator procedure, CLI command, or runbook step for setting it — leaving the coordination claim unverifiable and the operator without a concrete action.
findings.location: AC line 4: 'Run coordinated with live writers (reconciler pass paused during remediation)'
findings.suggested_fix: Document the gate-set step: e.g., `git update-ref refs/reconciler/gate $(echo 'OBSERVE' | git hash-object --stdin -w) <current-oid>` using the phase-gate mechanism already in _advisory_lock.py, then the release step after repair.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a mandatory batch size (e.g. `--limit=N` with a sleep between batches) for the repair run so the store is not held exclusively for the entire 2400+ ticket corpus at once.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no batching or throttling for the ~2422-ticket repair run, meaning the entire corpus could be processed in one uninterrupted shot. compact_all_cli does have a --limit flag, but the plan never mandates its use, leaving open a single large operation that holds the write lock across thousands of git commits and blocks concurrent store access for an extended period.
findings.location: What / Scope / AC bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the run plan: 'Execute in batches of ≤200 tickets via `fsck --repair --limit=200`; wait ≥30s between batches to allow queued writes to drain.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific pause mechanism for the reconciler (e.g., 'set `refs/reconciler/gate` via `_advisory_lock.check_phase_gate`', a GHA workflow dispatch flag, or a documented `rebar reconciler --pause` command) and include it in the runbook step for the live coordinated run.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the reconciler pass will be 'paused during remediation' (AC4, What section) but names no mechanism, CLI flag, environment variable, or runbook step for achieving this. Searching the codebase finds no `REBAR_RECONCILER_PAUSE`, `pause reconciler`, or `reconciler disable` surface. The existing `refs/reconciler/gate` / `check_phase_gate` mechanism in `_advisory_lock.py` could accomplish this, but the plan does not reference it.
findings.location: AC4 / What section ('run it against the live store with the 20-min reconciler pass paused') / src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_advisory_lock.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a work item in What/Scope for the orphan triage workflow: specify how LWW-scalar orphans are surfaced to a human (e.g., fsck --report output), what the decision options are, and where decisions are recorded (e.g., a triage log file or annotated fsck report).
findings.finding: AC#3 implies a human-triage workflow and a decision-recording mechanism (process + artifact) that is a distinct deliverable, but the What and Scope sections describe only automated repair tooling (fsck --repair, compact.py). There is no described work covering how the triage is conducted, what tool or interface is used, or where decisions are recorded.
findings.location: AC#3 vs What / Scope sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default record location and allowed decisions for LWW-scalar orphan triage in AC3 — e.g. 'decision recorded as a comment in this ticket or a named ops runbook, choosing one of: (a) hard-delete, (b) quarantine to a named bucket, (c) manual recover with a patch event.'
findings.finding: AC3's 'LWW-scalar orphan triage' outcome 'decision recorded' defers a real design decision to the executor without specifying the decision space (recover vs. hard-delete vs. quarantine) or the artifact where the decision is recorded, leaving it unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Decision recorded in the PR description or a linked ops doc; permitted outcomes: hard-delete (annotate count), quarantine to `orphan-triage/` prefix, or manual patch-event recovery. Chosen option and affected event IDs noted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC#2 and AC#3: either (a) clarify that AC#2's zero target is contingent on AC#3 human decisions resolving all orphans, or (b) restate AC#2 to 'zero unresolved ORPHAN_EVENT' where 'resolved' includes documented deferral decisions.
findings.finding: AC#2 demands the live store reaches 0 ORPHAN_EVENT, but AC#3 explicitly routes LWW-scalar orphans to human triage with only a 'decision recorded' outcome — no guarantee they are resolved. A human could legitimately decide to defer or leave some orphans, violating AC#2's zero-count target.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#2 vs AC#3
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC#2 to: 'Live store reaches 0 unresolved SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE, where orphans triaged and documented per AC#3 count as resolved.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC #2 or AC #3 whether 'reaches 0 ORPHAN_EVENT' requires human triage to be complete and actioned before this ticket is closed, or whether the acceptable end-state is 'additive orphans recovered + LWW-scalar orphans triaged with decision recorded (even if deferred)'.
findings.finding: AC #2 asserts 'live store reaches 0 ORPHAN_EVENT' as a deliverable of this plan, but AC #3 gates the deletion of LWW-scalar orphans on human triage and a recorded decision — an out-of-band action not guaranteed within this plan's execution. If triage concludes that some LWW-scalar orphans must be hard-deleted manually, the plan cannot autonomously deliver zero ORPHAN_EVENT, making the end-state proof in AC #2 potentially unverifiable at plan completion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #2 and AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC #2 to: 'SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT and MISSING_CREATE reach 0; ORPHAN_EVENT reaches 0 for additive orphans; LWW-scalar orphan count documented and disposition decision recorded per AC #3.' This makes the observable proof unambiguous regardless of the human triage outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the concurrency fence for the repair window: either pause all writers for affected snapshot IDs (or the entire store) during repair, or use optimistic concurrency (read event-log version, write snapshot only if version unchanged, else retry) to prevent silent event loss.
findings.finding: The plan pauses only the reconciler but does not address concurrent live writers that may append events to a snapshot being rebuilt, creating a TOCTOU race: the repair tool reads the event log, computes a new snapshot, then writes it — but a live writer could append between read and write, causing the rebuilt snapshot to silently omit the new event.
findings.location: AC line 4 / Scope — 'coordinated with live writers (reconciler pass paused)'
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer per-snapshot optimistic locking: record the event-log tail offset when starting rebuild, and use a compare-and-swap write that fails if the offset has advanced; retry the rebuild for that snapshot with the updated log. Alternatively, document that the repair window requires a brief write-pause (not just reconciler pause).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the dual-write window: either require a full write freeze (not just reconciler pause) for the repair window, or specify how writes arriving mid-repair are detected and re-folded (e.g. re-run fsck after repair to catch any new orphans created during the run).
findings.finding: The plan pauses only the reconciler pass during remediation but does not address concurrent user writes (rebar create/comment/tag/transition commands) that continue during the repair window. A new event written to a ticket between the event-log read and the new SNAPSHOT write would be excluded from the rebuilt snapshot, creating a fresh SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT or ORPHAN_EVENT entry immediately after repair.
findings.location: AC bullet 4 — 'Run coordinated with live writers (reconciler pass paused during remediation)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the repair architecture (`.retired`-aware rebuild, A2 coupling, orphan disposition policy) in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update, and document the orphan classification rule (additive vs LWW-scalar) as a configurable/reviewable policy rather than an implicit code constant.
findings.finding: The repair path introduces a novel cross-component dependency (fsck/compact reading `.retired` files via A1 machinery + A2 rebuild machinery) and codifies a new business rule (orphan disposition policy: additive vs LWW-scalar). Neither the architectural coupling nor the rule is captured in an ADR or design document.
findings.location: Scope / AC line 3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground 'A2's rebuild machinery' in a concrete module/function name and explicitly specify how the repair path extends it to include `*.retired` files in the event scan (since `reduce_ticket` and `compact_all_cli` both exclude them today).
findings.finding: The plan claims to reuse 'A2's rebuild machinery' for the repair path, but neither 'A2' nor its concrete module/function is identified. The nearest existing rebuild machinery (`compact.py`'s `_compact_locked` / `compact_all_cli`) explicitly EXCLUDES `*.retired` files — `reduce_ticket` in `reducer/_api.py:49` documents 'Excludes *.retired files' and the compact loop at `compact.py:70-73` filters on `*.json` without `*.retired`. AC#1 requires reading `.retired`; satisfying it requires new logic not present in the claimed reuse target, which the plan does not describe.
findings.location: Scope section ('reusing A2's rebuild machinery') and AC#1 ('reads `.retired`')
findings.suggested_fix: Name the A2 module/function explicitly. If the intent is to extend `compact_all_cli`, describe the patch: before calling `reduce_ticket`, enumerate `{ticket_dir}/*.retired` and surface them to the reducer or pre-rename them temporarily. If A2 delivers a new `rebuild_from_full_log()` function, cite its API here.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that *.retired files produced by A1 are in a format the repair path can ingest; if so, add explicit logic to include them in the replay (the current reducer and compact machinery excludes all *.retired files by design).
findings.finding: The plan's core repair premise — 'reads *.retired from A1' — is unverifiable and likely refuted. The reducer (reduce_ticket via _cache.py) globs only *.json files and explicitly EXCLUDES *.retired (reducer/_api.py lines 55, 80). No machinery in compact.py or fsck.py reads *.retired files as part of a rebuild. If A1 produces *.retired files and they must be included in the repair replay, the existing reducer/compact machinery will silently omit them.
findings.location: Scope section: 'reads .retired from A1'; AC line 1: 'Repair path rebuilds snapshots from the full ordered log (reads .retired)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the repair tool (fsck --repair or a dedicated script), before calling reduce_ticket(), temporarily rename or symlink *.retired events back to *.json (or implement a parallel replay that accepts a custom file list including *.retired), so the full ordered log is actually replayed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether A2's rebuild machinery (compact.py) can handle MISSING_CREATE / error-status tickets; if not (as the code shows it aborts on these), specify a separate repair path for those cases.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reusing A2's rebuild machinery' for snapshot rebuilding, but no dedicated rebuild path exists in compact.py or elsewhere that handles corrupted/inconsistent snapshots differently from normal compaction. compact.py's _compact_locked() aborts on status 'error' or 'fsck_needed' (line 106-108), which is exactly the status that MISSING_CREATE tickets would have — meaning A2's machinery cannot process the tickets that most need repair.
findings.location: Scope section: 'reusing A2's rebuild machinery'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that `fsck --repair` is NEW functionality to be added to fsck.py (or a new script), not an existing flag; specify its interface (flag name, behavior, safety guarantees) in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan refers to an 'fsck --repair' flag as the implementation vehicle, but no such flag exists anywhere in fsck.py or the broader codebase. fsck.py's fsck_cli() accepts only --output; it is explicitly documented as 'non-destructive'. The plan does not specify whether --repair is to be added to fsck.py, or is a separate script, leaving the implementation ambiguous.
findings.location: Scope section: 'an `fsck --repair`-style rebuild in `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` / `compact.py`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete mechanism for pausing the reconciler (e.g. an env flag, a ref-based gate, or a documented cron disable) and add any new file targets it requires to the Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan requires the reconciler pass to be 'paused during remediation' (AC-4), but there is no existing pause/disable mechanism (`reconciler.disable`, `REBAR_RECONCILER_DISABLE`, `--dry-run` pass-gate, etc.) anywhere in the codebase, and no file or procedure is named in the plan for implementing or invoking this coordination.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC-4 ('reconciler pass paused during remediation') / Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the repair path reads `*.retired` files: since `reduce_ticket()` currently excludes them, the plan must describe the new code path (e.g. a variant reducer that unions live `.json` + `.json.retired` files before replay) and confirm it is part of the deliverable.
findings.finding: The plan states the repair path 'reads `.retired`' as if this is an existing capability of A2's rebuild machinery, but `reduce_ticket()` in `reducer/_api.py` does NOT read `*.retired` files — `prepare_event_files` uses `glob.glob('*.json')` and `os.listdir` filtering on `endswith('.json')`, neither of which matches files ending in `.json.retired`. Reading `.retired` files is new, unspecified, and unverified code that must be written and validated.
findings.location: AC#1 ('Repair path rebuilds snapshots from the full ordered log (reads `.retired`)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the What/Scope section explaining why the chosen repair approach (full event-log rebuild) is preferred over alternatives such as targeted deletion of surviving source files or re-running `compact --threshold=0` on affected tickets.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing 'rebuild snapshot from full ordered event log' over the structurally simpler alternative of (a) deleting surviving source files already captured in each affected SNAPSHOT (direct targeted fix, reuses compact.py's existing deletion step) or (b) re-running `compact --threshold=0` on each affected ticket under the write lock (full reuse of A2 machinery including lock, I9 safety, and correct deletion). Without a rationale, implementers cannot evaluate trade-offs or catch if the chosen approach is wrong.
findings.location: What section / Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is to reuse A2's machinery: state explicitly that `rebar compact --threshold=0 --skip-sync` (or a batch variant) will be run on each affected ticket, which naturally re-reads the full event log, rebuilds the SNAPSHOT correctly under the write lock, and deletes surviving source files — avoiding the need to write new repair code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or explicitly design) the seam by which the repair path reads `.retired` files alongside `.json` events into one ordered replay, since the existing A2 machinery (`compact_all_cli`, `reduce_ticket`) explicitly excludes `*.retired` — document the specific code path or new module that bridges this gap before implementation begins.
findings.finding: The plan claims the repair path 'reuses A2's rebuild machinery' to rebuild snapshots from `*.retired` files, but the existing A2 machinery (`compact_all_cli` / `compact_cli` in `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py`) globs only `*.json` files and explicitly excludes `.retired`-suffixed files. The reducer (`prepare_event_files` in `reducer/_cache.py:102`) likewise globs `*.json` only. Reading `.retired` files as part of an ordered replay is a novel, unimplemented seam — the capability claim is unverified against the actual codebase.
findings.location: Scope section: 'reusing A2's rebuild machinery'; AC#1: 'Repair path rebuilds snapshots from the full ordered log (reads `.retired`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Describe a concrete repair seam: e.g., a new `_repair_ticket(ticket_dir)` that (1) discovers all `{ts}-{uuid}-{TYPE}.retired` files in the ticket dir alongside the live `.json` events, (2) strips the `.retired` suffix in memory to recover the original filename sort key for ordering, (3) feeds the merged sorted list into `replay_events`, and (4) writes a new SNAPSHOT via the existing `canonical_str` + `atomic_write` path. This is distinct from `compact_all_cli` and must be called out explicitly so implementers don't accidentally skip the `.retired` constituent events.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art / alternatives section to the plan that compares the chosen bespoke `fsck --repair` approach against at least one established event-sourced snapshot-rebuild pattern (e.g., Akka Persistence gap recovery, git-fsck repair flow), and records why the chosen design is preferred for this store's constraints.
findings.finding: The plan does not cite or compare any established prior art for event-sourced snapshot-rebuild / store-repair patterns before committing to a bespoke `fsck --repair` design. Systems with directly analogous corruption-remediation flows (git's own `git fsck --recover`, Akka Persistence's snapshot-gap recovery, Riak's AAE repair) have well-documented trade-offs (idempotency, partial-repair atomicity, live-writer coordination) that the plan does not address.
findings.location: What / Scope sections; no 'prior art' or 'alternatives considered' section present

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what code change makes the repair path include `*.retired` files — either a new `reduce_ticket_with_retired()` variant, a flag on `prepare_event_files`, or explicit pre-processing that renames/copies `.retired` files before calling the existing reducer.
findings.finding: `reduce_ticket` (in `src/rebar/reducer/_api.py`) explicitly excludes `*.retired` files — its docstring states 'Excludes *.retired files' and `prepare_event_files` only lists `.json` files not matching `.retired`. AC1 asserts the repair path 'reads `.retired`' from A1 by 'reusing A2's rebuild machinery', but A2's machinery (compact.py / reduce_ticket) cannot read `.retired` files without modification. The plan does not name any code change to `reduce_ticket` or a new reducer variant.
findings.location: AC1 / Scope section ('reusing A2's rebuild machinery') / src/rebar/reducer/_api.py:49
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan section naming the seam: e.g., 'The repair path will call a new `reduce_ticket(include_retired=True)` variant that also globs `*.json.retired` (or whichever extension A1 produces), bypassing the retired-file exclusion in `prepare_event_files`.' Also state the exact file extension A1 produces for retired files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `fsck --repair` interface: flag name (verify it doesn't conflict with existing `--output` parsing), exit-code contract (0 = fully repaired, 1 = partial, 2 = error?), output format (text lines / JSON?), and whether the byte-parity test `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` must be updated.
findings.finding: The plan names `fsck --repair` as the target interface (Scope: 'an `fsck --repair`-style rebuild in `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` / `compact.py`') but the current `fsck_cli` only parses `--output` format flags via `parse_output`; no `--repair` flag exists or is parsed. The interface contract (flag name, exit codes, output format, whether it's additive to `_scan`) is entirely unspecified.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:328-373

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete protocol for reading *.retired files in the repair path: which glob pattern, how .retired filenames map to sort keys, whether reduce_ticket gains a flag or a new entry point is introduced, and how .retired events are distinguished from live events during replay.
findings.finding: AC1 references reading '*.retired' files as part of the repair path ('rebuilds snapshots from the full ordered log including *.retired from A1'), but no reading protocol for .retired files is defined in the plan, and the existing repair machinery (reduce_ticket / compact.py) explicitly does NOT read .retired files — making this a referenced-but-undefined processing extension with no co-located specification.
findings.location: AC1: 'Repair path rebuilds snapshots from the full ordered log (reads .retired).' / Scope: 'reusing A2's rebuild machinery'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
